_comment: PLACEHOLDER -- THESE ARE NOT THE ANALYSIS EDGES. The edges below are copied verbatim from config/cuts.json's /mixing/pool_grid (the CONFIGURATION PRODUCT EDGES, which are also what the DIS normalisation denominator N_DIS(Q2, xB) is histogrammed on). They are here so that the code, the tests and the tools have a well-formed, loadable Grid A to run against before any data has been scanned. They are round human numbers, NOT equal-statistics edges: nothing about them is fitted to the RG-D acceptance and no result may be quoted from them. REPLACE THIS FILE WHOLESALE with the output of the make_grid tool, computed from real data, before any production run. make_grid rewrites this file and fills in /provenance; do not hand-edit the edges.
_grid_comment: Grid A is the (Q2, xB) grid of the factorized binning (note sec:binning-future). It is combined with Grid B (z, pT2) as a PRODUCT: the 4D bin is (A cell) x (B cell), and Grid B is INDEPENDENT of the A cell. The flat index formula is documented in src/core/Binning.hpp and is what the downstream Python decodes against -- it is written down there rather than nowhere, which is the whole difference from the superseded kd-tree, whose leaf formula had to be reverse-engineered out of the output files.
_coincidence_warning: TRAP IN WAITING. These edges currently EQUAL cuts.json's /mixing/pool_grid edges. That is a temporary artefact of them both being placeholders, NOT a design invariant, and nothing may be built on it. The pool grid and the N_DIS denominator must stay identical TO EACH OTHER (see that block's _comment) and are hand-maintained; Grid A is machine-fitted to data and will diverge from both the moment make_grid runs. Code that assumes one grid can stand in for the other will be silently wrong on that day and correct on every day before it -- which is the worst possible failure schedule. src/core/Binning.cpp loads this file and never reads cuts.json.
_axes_order_comment: AXIS ORDER IS LOAD-BEARING. axes[0] is the SLOW axis of the row-major flat index (cell = i_q2 * n_xb + i_xb), axes[1] the FAST one. Binning::load() checks the names against the order it expects and refuses a swapped pair, because a swapped pair would bin, index and plot perfectly cleanly while labelling every result with the wrong variable.

axes.name: q2
axes._comment: Q^2 in GeV^2. 9 edges -> 8 bins. Strictly increasing; Binning::load() refuses anything else. The lower edge 1.0 is dis.q2_min from cuts.json -- below it there are no events by construction. The top edge 11.0 is ABOVE the kinematic maximum (Q2 ~= 9.6 GeV^2 at E_beam = 10.53 GeV), so the top bin [7.0, 11.0) is unbounded in practice: it is the bin the note's sec:binning-caveat is about, and it is exactly why nothing downstream may report a bin at its geometric midpoint.

axes.name: xb
axes._comment: x_B, dimensionless. 8 edges -> 7 bins. There is NO x_B cut in the analysis (see cuts.json /dis), so this grid genuinely truncates the data at both ends: events with x_B < 0.1 or x_B > 0.7 fall outside Grid A and Binning::find_4d() returns -1 for them. That is deliberate and must stay visible -- there are no flow bins here, and a caller that wants those events counted has to count the -1s itself.

provenance._comment: Where these edges came from. Read and type-checked by Binning::load() -- required, never defaulted -- so that it cannot decay into another declared-set-and-never-read key. It is deliberately NOT part of provenance_hash(): the hash covers the axis names and the edges only, so re-wording a comment here does not invalidate an existing result, while moving an edge by one ulp does.
provenance._source_comment: 'placeholder' until make_grid has run. make_grid replaces this with the identity of the scan that produced the edges (dataset, target, polarity, file list hash).
provenance._n_events_comment: 0 because no data was scanned to make these. A real equal-statistics grid records the number of events its quantiles were computed from -- that number is what makes the 'equal statistics' claim checkable.
